content.problemStatement:
A prison is represented as an N×MN\times MN×M grid containing `0`'s and `1`'s, where `0` denotes a prisoner and `1` denotes a guard.

In one move, a prisoner can move one step left, right, up, or down. Formally, from cell (x,y)(x, y)(x,y), a prisoner can move to any of the four cells (x−1,y),(x+1,y),(x,y−1),(x,y+1)(x-1, y), (x+1, y), (x, y-1), (x, y+1)(x−1,y),(x+1,y),(x,y−1),(x,y+1).

A prisoner can escape if he reaches outside the prison: formally, if he reaches any cell (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) such that x=0x = 0x=0 or y=0y = 0y=0 or x>Nx \gt Nx>N or y>My \gt My>M.

Let the escape time of a prisoner be the **minimum** number of guards he needs to cross before he is able to escape the prison.

What is the **maximum** escape time of any prisoner?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the dimensions of the prison.
The next NNN lines each contain a binary string of length MMM, representing the structure of the prison.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the maximum escape time of any prisoner.
constraints: Each character of grid is either `0` or `1`.
The sum of N⋅MN\cdot MN⋅M over all test cases won't exceed 3⋅1053\cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There is only one prisoner. No matter which direction he moves in, he'll encounter a guard.
Moving two steps right will let him escape the prison while only encountering one guard.
Test case 222: Every prisoner can escape without ever encountering a guard.
Test case 333: The prisoner at cell (3,3)(3, 3)(3,3) has an escape time of 222, while the others have escape times of 111. The answer is the maximum escape time, hence 222.
